summary:
Zoroaster (Zarathustra, traditionally c. 1000–600 BC, with scholarly dates ranging widely) was the Iranian prophet whose religious reforms established Zoroastrianism — one of the world's oldest monotheistic (or dualistic) religions, which profoundly influenced Judaism, Christianity, and Islam through its doctrines of cosmic dualism, individual moral judgment, resurrection, heaven and hell, and eschatological expectations. He is among history's most influential religious figures despite the limited direct evidence for his biography.

Zoroaster's reform of ancient Iranian polytheism elevated Ahura Mazda ('Wise Lord') as the supreme deity of truth and goodness in cosmic struggle against Angra Mainyu ('Destructive Spirit') — a radical ethical monotheism in which each human participates in this cosmic battle through moral choices, to be judged after death. The Gathas (Zoroaster's own hymns, preserved in the Avesta) are among the oldest religious texts in any Indo-European language. Zoroastrianism became the state religion of the Achaemenid Persian Empire (see [[event:1141|Zoroastrianism in Achaemenid]]), and its concepts of apocalyptic judgment, cosmic battle between good and evil, and individual afterlife significantly influenced the Jewish writings of the exilic period — texts that in turn shaped early Christianity and Islam.
